California
Fair Political
Practices Cornmission
James P. Corn
Turner and Sullivan
l-Ooo G street, suite 3oo
Sacramento, CA 9 5814
December 2'7, l99O
Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
our Fil,e No. I-9 0-4 3 4
Dear Mr. Corn:
This j,s in response to your letter requesting fo11ow-up
advice on behalf of the California Council of Civil- Engineers
regarding the responsibi lities of their nenbers under the
conf ]ict-of- interest provisions of the Political 
Reform Act (the
rras1rr1.1/ since your advice request refers to hypotheticat
governmental decisions, we are only able to,Provide informal
assistance with respect to your questj,ons.z/
In addition, please be alrare the commission's advice is
f irnited to the application of the law to a particular set of facts
provided by the requestor. This is because the application of the
provisions-of the ict is necessarily dependent on lhe specific
iacts and circurnstances surroundinq a specific decision.
OUESTTONS
1. What are the disqualif ication obligations of ernployees of
a engineering firrn who possess no ownership interest in the firrn
and who provide under contract, inforrnation, advice, recom-
mendation or counsel to a state or local government agency?
2. Will participation in or preparation of design services
for a governnental agency by an engineering firm's emPloyees
qualiff as participation in a governmental- decision under the Act?
L/ covernment code sections 81ooo-91015. Ail statutory
references are to the Governrnent code unless otherwise indicated.
cornmission regulations appear at 2 california code of Regulations
Section 18OOO; et seq. A11 references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 5 of the California Code of Regulations.
2/ rnformal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
i^.""iiy 
provided by an opinion or formal written advice' (section
83114; Regulation 18329 (c) (3). )
428 J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 8O7 o Sacramento CA 958O4.O8O7 o (916) 322'5660


Our File No. I-90-434
Page 2
3. Does a review by the governmental agency of the data
prepared by the consultant equate to i,ndependent substantive
review?
4. If a governmental agency contracts with a finn for
information, advice, reconmendation or counsel and recites in the
contract that the provision of services will not fall within the
definition of rrconsultanttr in the Act, can the firm's employees
avoid beingr consultants?
5. 
Can employees of a firrn be consultants where the firm's
compensatj,on is paid by an entity other than the local
governnental agency?
CONCLUSIONS
1. Employees of an engineering firm, who are consultants but
do not possess any ownership interest in the fj.rm, have an
econonj-c interest in the firm which is a source of income to then,
and do not have an economic interest in the clients of the firn.
Accordingly, they must disqualify thenselves if a decision will
have a material financial effect on the firm.
2. Participation in or preParation of design services
constitutes parti,cipation in a governnental decision, irrespective
of whether such participation will foreseeably and naterially
affect a financial interest.
3. fndependent substantive review requires more than a
governmental agency merely reviewing the product provided by the
consultant; it requires revj-ew by the government agency
independent of the data or analysis Prepared by the consultant,
and independent checkinq of the data or analysis on lrhich the
conclusions were based.
4. 
Such a provision in the contract nay aid the parties in
settj.ng up a relationship vhich does not result in a person being
a consultant; however, the factual circumstances rrould determine
whether an engineer is deemed a consultant under the Act.
5. while the source of compensation is an indication of
whether the enployees have becone consuLtant6, it iS not
deterrninative.
FACTS
You have requested follow up advice with respect to our
l-etter to you of April 12, l99O (Corn Advice Letter, No. I-89-433)
and the respons ibi l ities of consultants under the conflict-of-


Our File No. I-90-434
Page 3
interest provisions of the Act. In our letter to you we advised
that section 871o0 prohibits any public official, 
including
consultants, from rnaking, participating in rnaking, or usj-ng his
official 
position to influence a governmentat decision in which he
knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest.
(section 82048. )
we also advised that because the consuLtants, employer is
both a business interest and a source of incorne, the consultants'
emptoyer is an economic interest of the consultant. In addition,
if the consultant is also an owner of the firm, sources of incone
to the firn, 
(i,e., clients) are then sources of j.ncome to the
consultant.
we further advised that:
tllf the engineer works with and discusses the
proposed design with the public agency enqineer,
thus giving the public agency engineer the
opportunity to conment upon, and provide input
regarding, the proposed design for the
improvements, the engineer would be deemed a
consultant. Under these circumstances the engineer
is not working independent of the control and
direction of the public agency engineer within the
rneaning of Regulation 18700(a) (2) (A). Similarly,
when the engineer is deterrnining the estirnated
costs of the proposed improvernents, and when he is
developing the nethodology and perforroing the
calculatj-ons for spreading the benefit within the
assessment district, 
the engineer is not working
independent of the control and direction of the
public agency engineer if he is seeking input and
comments fron the public agency engineer regarding
those tasks. Under such circumstances, the
engineer is acting rnuch like an employee of the
agency who is under the supervision of the public
agency engineer. In that situation, the engineer
is deerned a consultant and, therefore, a public
official 
within the rneaning of the Act.
***
[T]he exclusion would not apply if the
subconsultant, in fact, is subject to the control
and direction of the public agency engineer.
DISCUSSION
The Political Reform Act sras enacted by the people of the
State of California by initiative 
in 1974. The purpose for the


Our File No. 
■-90-434
Page 4
conf l ict-of- j,nterest provisions of the Act was to ensure that
public officials, 
whether elected or appointed, would Perform
their duties j,n an impartial manner, free frorn bias caused by
their own financial interests or the financia] interests of
persons r^rho have supported them. (section 81001(b).)
In furtherance of this goal, section 87100 of the Act
prohibits any public official 
frorn making, participating in mak-
ing, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence
a governnental decision j-n which the official 
has a financial
interest. 
You have requested foIlov, up advice with respect to the
disqual i f ication obligations of consultants under the Act. 
You
have provided specific guestions concernj.ng the advice rendered in
that previ,ous advice letter.
■.fnterest in the Firm.
If the employees of an engineering firm are consultants and,
therefore, puf:-ic officials 
under the Act, they would be required
to disqualiiy thernselves from participating in any decision if it
is reaJonably foreseeable that the decision will have a naterial
financial- effect on an econornic interest. 
(Section 87103.)
cLearly, the employees would have an econornic interest in their
employer. (Section 87103(c) and (d).) 
In addition, where an
emp:-oyee is aLso an owner of a 1Ot or greater interest in one of
th- firms, section 82030 provides that the a pro-rata share of the
income to the firm is treated as di.rect income to the individual;
thus, the client is a source of income to the employee for
purposes of the Act. 
(Webb Advice Letter, No. I-89-415, copy
enclosed. ) However, where an employee has I'ess than a 1ot
ownership interest in the f irrn, the only source of incone will be
the firn.
You have suggested in your request for follolr-up advice that
the 10* rufe enunciated in Section 82030 unfairly discrirninates
against snaller firms. While the 108 rule may generally apply
more often to smalter business entities, in fact the rule is a
reflection of the distinction betvreen a person with an ownership
interest in a business and one srho merely works for the business.
The ovrner has a right to more than just a salary, an oe/ner
generally has a rj,qht to sone oortion of the business itself,
includinq income and assets. This rule is reflected in the same
manner in the definition of rrinvestrnentrr and Itinterest in real
property" under the Act. 
(Section 82033;' Section 82034.)
2. Desiqn Services
As stated above, the Act was intended to ensure that public
officials 
wouJ.d perforn their duties in an impartial manner, free
from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial


Our File No. 
■-90-434
Page 5
interests of persons who have supported them. (section 810o1(b).)
The Act has been broadly interpreted to prevent evasion of the
conf I ict-of-interest 
safeguards by sinple del-egration of decision-
making authority to private parties such as consultants or
independent contractors, or private entities such as private
boards created by a governnental agency. (See e.q., fn re Malonev
(L977) 3 FPPC Ops. 69; In re Siegel (L977) 3 FPPC ops, 52, copies
enclosed. )
In implementing the conf lict-of-interest 
provj-sions of the
Act, the comrnission has develoPed, by statutory interpretation,
regulation, and opinion, a standard analysis in determining !,Ihen
disqual ification is required. Each factor in the analvsis is
1. Is the decision naker a public official?
2. Is the public official 
making, participating in rnaking, or
otherwise using his or her official 
position to influence a
governmentaL decision?
3. Does the official 
have an econonic interest in the decision?
4. fs it reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
financial effect on the official 
or a member of his or her
irnrnediate family or on the economic interest?
5. will the foreseeable effect be material?
6. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
financial effect on the official 
or a member of his or her
innediate fanily which is distinguishabLe from the effect on the
public generally?
It srould appear that generally, in the preparation of various
design services, an engineer exercises his or her discretion to
select fron different options available to him or her in preparingl
a particular design. fn exercising this discretion the engineer
$/ould be participallng in governmental deci.sions. (Regulation
18700(b) and (c).)3/ 
Such participation will not necessarily be
prohibited, if the participati.on will not have a foreseeable and
material financial effect on an economic interest. 'It is,
nevertheless, rrparticipationrt as defined in the Act.
3/ of course this nay not be true under all circurnstances. If in
the preparation of the various design services the engineer
exercises no discretion, the engineer would not be rtmakj,ng or
parti.ci.pating in the making of a governmental decisionI as defined
in Regulation 18700. Obviously, any conclusion would be dependent
on the facts of the particul-ar situation.


Our Fi■e NO. I-90-434
Page 6
3. Independent Substantive Review
If there is "significant intervenj-ng substantive reviewrr of
the enployees' recommendations by the governmental agency the
enployees of the firrn would not be participating in a governmental
decision and consequently would not be consul,tants under the Act.
(Regulation l-8700(c); Leidiqh Advice Letter, No. A-89-320, copy
enclosed. ) As stated in the initial 
letter to you, the concept of
rrindependent substantive reviewrr is narrowly applied. For
example, the commj-ssion deterrnined the exception was not
applicable where decisions are rnerely rrreviewedrr by the
governmental agency and the review by the agency is in reliance on
the data or analysis prepared by the consultant without checking
it independently, or in reliance on the professional judgment of
the consultant, or where the consultant in some other way actually
influences the final decision. 
(Kaplan Advice Letter,
No. A-82-108, copy enclosed. )
You have asked whether the plan check requirement satisfies
the standard for independent substantive review. Your initial
letter of JuIy, 1989 does not discuss the plan check requirement.
If the plan check requirement, or any other intervening review by
the government agency is not in reliance on the data or analysis
prepared by the consultant and involves independentlv checkinq the
conclusi,ons and data on which the conclusions were based, the
requirernent rnay satisfy the requirement of independent substantive
review. However, this is necessarily a factual question that must
be determined on a case by case basis. we have enclosed the
foJ,Iowing letters for guidance: Kaplan Advice Letter, No. A-82-
108; Rose Advice Letter, A-84-306 and A-84-299,' Leidioh Advice
Letter, No,89-320.
4. The Nature of the Requirements of Reoulation 18700(a) (2)
You suggested in Your letter that
18700(a) (2) in the contract between the
woul-d exclude the firn's employees from
Regulation 18700 (a) provides:
adoption of Regulation
f inn and the public agency
being consultants .
(a) "Public official 
at any level of state or
1oca1 government. means every natural person who is
a member, officer, employee, or consultant of a
state or local government agency.
(2) 'rConsultantrr shall include any
naturaL person rrho provides, under contract,
inforrnation, advice, recornmendation or counsel


Our File No. I-90-434
Page 7
to a state or LocaL government agency,
provided, however, that rrconsultant" shal1 not
include a person who:
(A) Conducts research and arrj.ves
at conclusions with respect to his or her
rendition of i,nf orrnation, advice, recom-
mendation, or counsel independent of
control and direction of the agency or
any agency official, 
other than nornal
contract rnonitoring; and
(B) Possesses no authority with
respect to any agency decision beyond the
rendition of information, advice, recom-
mendation or counsel.
(Enphasis added. )
ctearty the exception in 18700(a) (2) is a fact based
exception, and not one which can be net by a mere recital- in a
contract. For an eroployee to be exernpted he or she rnust conduct
necessary research and arrive at conclusions indeDendent of control
and direction of the agency or any agency official g4! possess no
authority with respect to any agency decision beyond the rendition
of inforrnation, adviee, recommendation or counsel . Thus, a
contractual recital night aid the parties in setting up a
relationship which does not result in the employee being considered
a rrconsultantrr,' however, the factual circunstances lroul,d deterrnine
$/hether the engineer is deened a consultant under the Act.
5. source of comDensation to the Firm
Your final question concerns the source of cornpensation as a
determj-native test for whether a specific engineer will be a
consultant under the Act. As discussed above and in our previous
letter to you, Regulation 18700(a) defines the term rrconsuLtantf as
fo I lows :
(2) trConsultant" shall- include any natural
person who provides, under contract, information,
advice, reconmendation or counsel to a state or
local government agency. . .
The source of compensation is not determinative. However, if
a engineer has no contract with the government agency to perform
work, we have advised that he is not a consultant under Regulation
18700(a)(2). (Olsen Advice Letter, No. I-90-451, copy enclosed.)
of course, a contract may be oraL as well as written.


Our Fi■e No. 
■-90-434
Page 8
I trust this letter has addressed your concerns. Should you
have any further questions regarding this matter or a specific
decision that you would like advice on, please feel free to contact
me at (916) 322-59oL.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting ceneral Counsel

John w. WaLlace
counsel, Legal Division

relationship which does not result in the employee being considered
a rrconsultantrr,' however, the factual circunstances lroul,d deterrnine
$/hether the engineer is deened a consultant under the Act.
5. source of comDensation to the Firm
Your final question concerns the source of cornpensation as a
determj-native test for whether a specific engineer will be a
consultant under the Act. As discussed above and in our previous
letter to you, Regulation 18700(a) defines the term rrconsuLtantf as
fo I lows :
(2) trConsultant" shall- include any natural
person who provides, under contract, information,
advice, reconmendation or counsel to a state or
local government agency. . .
The source of compensation is not determinative. However, if
a engineer has no contract with the government agency to perform
work, we have advised that he is not a consultant under Regulation
18700(a)(2). (Olsen Advice Letter, No. I-90-451, copy enclosed.)
of course, a contract may be oraL as well as written.


Our Fi■e No. 
■-90-434
I trust this letter has addressed your concerns. Should you
have any further questions regarding this matter or a specific
decision that you would like advice on, please feel free to contact
me at (916) 322-59oL.

3. Does a review by the governmental agency of the data

4. If a governmental agency contracts with a finn for
information, advice, reconmendation or counsel and recites in the
contract that the provision of services will not fall within the

avoid beingr consultants?
5. 
Can employees of a firrn be consultants where the firm's
compensatj,on is paid by an entity other than the local
governnental agency?

CONCLUSION: 1. Employees of an engineering firm, who are consultants but
do not possess any ownership interest in the fj.rm, have an
econonj-c interest in the firm which is a source of income to then,
and do not have an economic interest in the clients of the firn.
Accordingly, they must disqualify thenselves if a decision will
have a material financial effect on the firm.
2. Participation in or preParation of design services
constitutes parti,cipation in a governnental decision, irrespective
of whether such participation will foreseeably and naterially
affect a financial interest.
3. fndependent substantive review requires more than a
governmental agency merely reviewing the product provided by the
consultant; it requires revj-ew by the government agency
independent of the data or analysis Prepared by the consultant,
and independent checkinq of the data or analysis on lrhich the
conclusions were based.
